behaviors.description: Migrations are applied in ascending numeric order determined by the filename prefix.
behaviors.justification: Failure is at a module handoff (filesystem discovery -> sort -> runner execution order). A unit test of sort logic alone can miss bugs in discovery/filtering/wiring. No real OS/network/subprocess requirement beyond local filesystem and wired modules.
behaviors.test_description: Create a temp `migrations/` directory with out-of-order files (for example `10_add.sql`, `2_seed.sql`, `1_init.sql`). Run the real runner with a DB adapter that records execution sequence, and assert statements execute in numeric prefix order `1,2,10`.

behaviors.description: A migration that has already been applied is not re-executed on a subsequent run.
behaviors.justification: Real failure appears in interaction between migration execution and `_migrations` metadata persistence across runs. Mocking one side can hide state/wiring defects. No LLM or multi-step agentic dependency.
behaviors.test_description: Run the runner once on a temp DB with one migration that mutates observable state and records in `_migrations`. Run the runner again against the same DB and assert the migration side effect did not occur twice and metadata still has one record for that version.

behaviors.description: When a migration statement fails, its changes are rolled back automatically and no further migrations in the same run are attempted.
behaviors.justification: This depends on real PostgreSQL transactional behavior under failure (rollback semantics) plus runner control flow stop-on-error. Transaction mocks can falsely pass and hide real DB semantics issues.
behaviors.test_description: Use a real Postgres instance. Provide migration N with two statements where the second fails after the first writes data, followed by migration N+1 with a detectable side effect. Run runner and assert: (1) no partial data from migration N remains, (2) migration N is not recorded as applied, and (3) migration N+1 was not executed.

behaviors.description: When two instances of the runner start at the same time against the same database, each migration is applied exactly once across both instances.
behaviors.justification: Behavior explicitly requires concurrent separate processes and DB locking/uniqueness guarantees under race. This is an OS/process scheduling + real DB concurrency boundary; integration mocks typically miss these races.
behaviors.test_description: Start a real Postgres and launch two runner processes simultaneously against the same migrations set. Use a migration with an observable idempotence-sensitive effect. After both exit, assert each migration version appears exactly once in `_migrations` and side effects occurred exactly once.

behaviors.description: On the very first run against a fresh database, the `_migrations` table is created before any migration file is applied.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is startup sequencing between metadata bootstrap and migration execution. Requires modules wired with a fresh DB state, but not OS-level concurrency or external AI behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Against a fresh test DB, include a first migration that would fail if `_migrations` does not yet exist (for example, references it). Run runner and assert success plus `_migrations` exists before migration effects are committed, proving bootstrap preceded file application.
